title: How to rebuild a relationship with food after trauma
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your body isn't broken. Your nervous system is just trying to keep you alive. 

 I remember standing in my kitchen at 11 p.m., staring into the refrigerat...
htmlContent:
Your body isn't broken. Your nervous system is just trying to keep you alive.

I remember standing in my kitchen at 11 p.m., staring into the refrigerator. I wasn't hungry. I wasn't even tasting the food I was shoving into my mouth. I was just trying to make something stop. That feeling. That buzzing, humming, unbearable aliveness inside my own skin. And for a few seconds, the chewing worked. The crunching. The swallowing. It drowned out the noise. Until it didn't.

Here's the thing about trauma and food. We don't usually start with a conscious decision to have a disordered relationship with eating. It creeps in. It's a survival strategy that your brain stumbled onto one day when you were drowning and it happened to work. Food becomes medication. Control. Punishment. Comfort. Distraction. It becomes the one thing you can actually manage when everything else feels like chaos.

I spent years believing I just lacked willpower. That I was broken in some fundamental way that other people weren't. That everyone else had figured out this eating thing and I was the only one still struggling. I wasn't. And neither are you.

Trauma lives in the body. Bessel van der Kolk said it plainly in The Body Keeps the Score - the body remembers what the mind tries to forget. And your relationship with food is one of the most intimate, repetitive, daily interactions you have with your own body. Of course trauma shows up there. Where else would it go?

The nervous system is the real driver here, not your lack of discipline

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us something crucial. Your nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. It's doing this below your conscious awareness, all day every day. When it detects threat - and trauma survivors have hypersensitive threat detection - it shifts you into survival mode. Fight, flight, freeze, or fawn.

And here's where food comes in. When you're in a sympathetic state (fight or flight), your body shuts down digestion. You might feel nauseous. You might restrict. You might feel wired and unable to eat. That's your body saying "we don't have time for this, we need to survive."

When you're in a dorsal vagal state (freeze, collapse, shutdown), your metabolism slows. You might feel numb. You might binge without tasting. You might reach for sugar because it's the fastest way to get a temporary spike in energy and dopamine. That's your body saying "we're going under, grab whatever fuel you can."

Neither of these is a character flaw. They're nervous system responses. They're your body doing exactly what it evolved to do. The problem is that your threat detection system got stuck in the "on" position. And food became one of the only tools you had to regulate yourself.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did you feel a wave of recognition mixed with shame? Stay with me here. That shame is part of the pattern. It's the voice that tells you you're doing it wrong. That you should be better by now. That everyone else has this figured out.

That voice is also a trauma response. It's the part of you that learned to survive by being hard on yourself before anyone else could be harder.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, explains that every part of you - even the part that binges, the part that restricts, the part that judges you for both - is trying to help. They're just using old strategies that don't work anymore.

Restriction and bingeing are two sides of the same coin

Here's something that took me way too long to understand. Restriction and bingeing aren't opposites. They're partners. They're a cycle. And trauma is what keeps the cycle spinning.

When you restrict - whether it's calories, food groups, meal times, or portions - your body registers that as a threat. Scarcity. Danger. Your nervous system says "we don't know when we're getting food again, so when it shows up, we need to consume as much as possible." That's biology. That's not weakness. That's your survival brain doing its job.

Then you binge. And then the shame hits. And the shame drives more restriction. And the cycle continues. And you feel like you're going crazy because you can't seem to stop something that you know is hurting you.

Wild, right? The thing you're using to cope is also the thing that's keeping you stuck.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about trauma and the body, talks about how the survival strategies we developed in childhood become the problems we struggle with in adulthood. The restriction or bingeing or emotional eating that kept you alive then is now causing you pain. But your brain doesn't know the difference. It's still running the old program.

So what do you do? You don't try to rip the program out. That creates more threat. More dysregulation. More of the cycle. Instead, you start by just noticing. You build observation without judgment. You watch the cycle without jumping in to fix it.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic helped me understand that my food struggles weren't separate from my trauma history. They were part of the same wound.

Rebuilding trust with your body starts with small, boring, consistent actions

I know you want the big transformation. The dramatic breakthrough. The moment where everything clicks and suddenly you're free. I wanted that too. I waited for it for years. It didn't come.

What came instead was slow. Boring. Unspectacular. I started by eating breakfast at the same time every day. That's it. Just one meal, at roughly the same time, every day. My nervous system needed to learn that food was reliable. That it would show up. That it didn't need to panic.

Your body has been in survival mode for a long time. It doesn't trust you yet. Why would it? You've been fighting against it, judging it, trying to control it. The way you rebuild trust with a frightened animal is not by yelling at it. It's by showing up, consistently, with food and safety and patience.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation - the practice of moving between activation and settling. You can do this with food too. You eat something. You notice how your body feels. You pause. You breathe. You eat a little more. You stop when your body says stop. You don't ignore the signals. You practice hearing them.

This is going to feel wrong at first. Your brain is going to scream at you that you're doing it wrong. That you need more rules. More structure. More control. That's the trauma talking. That's the part of you that learned to survive by being rigid. You don't have to listen to it. You just have to keep showing up.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work on polyvagal theory offers exercises that help you build capacity for regulation. And regulation is what makes it possible to have a different relationship with food.

You can't think your way out of a dysregulated nervous system. You have to practice your way out. Bite by bite. Breath by breath. Day by day.

The shame is the cage. The food is just the lock.

I'm going to say something uncomfortable. You might not be ready for it. That's okay. You can come back to it later.

The food isn't really the problem. I know it feels like it is. I know you've spent years obsessing over what you eat, when you eat, how much you eat. I know you've tried every diet, every protocol, every app. I know you've blamed yourself for not being able to stick with any of them.

But the food is a symptom. The real problem is the shame. The real problem is the belief that you're flawed. That you need to be fixed. That your body is wrong and needs to be controlled into submission.

That belief didn't come from nowhere. It came from trauma. From a world that didn't keep you safe. From caregivers who couldn't meet your needs. From a culture that profits from your self-hatred. You absorbed it like a sponge absorbs water. And now it's the lens through which you see everything, including food.

So here's the work. It's not about finding the perfect diet. It's not about finally having enough willpower. It's about questioning the shame. It's about noticing when the inner critic shows up and saying "that's not truth, that's an old recording." It's about feeding yourself with the same compassion you would offer a scared child.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helped me see that my struggles with food weren't about food at all. They were about never learning that I deserved to have my needs met.

You deserve to have your needs met. Including the need for nourishment. Including the need for pleasure. Including the need for food that tastes good and feels good in your body.

I'm not saying it's easy. I'm not saying you'll never struggle again. I still have days where I eat standing over the sink, not tasting anything, trying to make the feelings go away. But those days are farther apart now. And when they happen, I don't pile shame on top of them. I just notice. I breathe. I try again at the next meal.

That's the whole thing. It's not about getting it right. It's about staying in relationship with yourself, even when it's messy. Even when you don't trust yourself. Even when the old patterns scream louder than anything else.

Sit with that. Let it land. Your relationship with food isn't a problem to be solved. It's a relationship to be healed. And healing takes time. It takes patience. It takes showing up, over and over, even when you don't believe it's working.

You're not broken. You're not behind. You're not doing it wrong. You're just a person with a traumatized nervous system, trying to find your way back to your body. And that's hard work. Honest work. The kind of work that doesn't get celebrated but should.

Keep going. One meal at a time.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A validating look at how childhood dynamics shape adult struggles.
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to nervous system regulation and building felt safety.
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's resource for understanding and healing C-PTSD.
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's gentle introduction to Internal Family Systems and parts work.

As an Amazon Associate, I earn from qualifying purchases.
